She looked at Max.

"You still have that dead med-bay locker key from tutorial lot?"

Max blinked.

"I thought it was trash."

"Nothing is trash in a place with memory erosion."

Max pulled the key from his belt pouch.

Small, metal, analog.

The kind of object systems hate because they can't monitor it without admitting they're afraid of simple things.

Munch closed his hand around it.

"Locker B-17, lower med corridor. We cache copies there."

Maxine checked the hallway.

"Do it now. If cache decays, we did all this for a bedtime story."

Max nodded and pushed off the wall.

His muscles ached like old code.
His head buzzed.
His HUD was crowded.

But his hands were steady.

He moved.

Not because the system told him to.

Because the timer was real.

Because the files were real.

Because one hard-won fact had become three images and a process tag and that was enough to build a future problem.

In Sector 7, that counted as hope.

He passed under the cycle board and did not look up.

Ahead: med corridor.
Locker B-17.
Five hours to keep proof alive.

Behind him: Maxine and Munch fell into step, one on each side, like bodyguards or accomplices or both.

No speeches.
No promises.

Just movement through steel.

Groundhog day.

And this time, he carried evidence.

Med corridor B was colder than the rest of the ring and smelled like antiseptic over rust.

Half the light strips were dead.
The working half hummed like insects.

Locker B-17 sat in a row of old emergency cabinets with chipped paint and manual latch plates, relics from whatever safety policy existed before The Company discovered euphemisms.

Max knelt, inserted the analog key, and turned.

The lock resisted.

Then gave.

Inside: empty shelf, one torn glove, one cracked med patch, and enough space for three pieces of proof no system should lose.

Munch handed Max a wafer-thin storage shim from inside her suit sleeve.

"Transfer."

Max pushed the capture files to local external.

Progress bar crawled.

10%.
27%.
49%.

Maxine stood at corridor mouth, helmet tilted, listening.

"Two drones incoming from north lane. Thirty seconds."

Max didn't look up.

74%.
88%.
94%.

The transfer stalled.

A warning pane blinked:

[TRANSFER ERROR]
Tracked token interference detected.
Retry? Y/N

"You've got to be kidding me."

Munch crouched beside him and slapped a patch cable into the side of Max's wrist module.

"Hard line. Bypass token mediation."

"You can do that?"

"For six seconds."

Maxine's voice sharpened.

"Fifteen seconds to contact."

Max hit RETRY.

The bar jumped.

96%.
99%.

Then:

[TRANSFER COMPLETE]
3 files copied.
Integrity loss window suspended (offline media).

Munch yanked the cable free and shoved the shim into the locker's back seam behind a loose panel screw.

She replaced the torn glove over the hiding spot and closed the door.

Key out.
Pocket.

Just as two patrol drones rolled into corridor line-of-sight.

Their lenses swept.
Paused.

One projected a neutral prompt in the air:

[ROUTINE CHECK]
State purpose in Med Corridor B.

Maxine answered before Max could.

"Looking for a bandage."

The drone processed.

Munch held up her gloved hand where she'd sliced a fingertip earlier on conduit metal. A thin dark line had dried across the knuckle.

"Found one," she said.

The drone lens adjusted focus, verified visible injury, and logged:

[ROUTINE CHECK]
Medical self-service acknowledged.
Proceed.

The drones rolled on.

No sirens.
No escalation.

When they disappeared around the bend, Max exhaled so hard his shoulders hurt.

Maxine looked back down corridor and spoke without turning.

"We got lucky."

Munch shook her head.

"No. We got boring."

Max touched the pocket where the key sat and thought of the shim hidden behind a torn glove.

Proof offline.

For now.

His HUD opened one final pane for the night.

[PERSONAL NOTE - UNSAVED]
not first max
delta mirror router
don't lose locker key

Max did not dismiss it.

He left it open while they walked back into station noise and shift churn and the endless machinery of paid survival.

Somewhere high above, the cycle board clicked to the next countdown.

Somewhere deep below, a process named DELTA_MIRROR_ROUTER kept running.

And somewhere in an old locker in a cold corridor, three blurry images waited for the next bad decision.

Debt Protocol is long-form fiction set in the world of Sector Scavengers: Signal & Salvage.
